content.full_text:
STATE OF CALI FORNI A 
FAIR l•OLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite anon• Sacramento, CA 95811 
( 9 16) :3 2 2 -.5 6 6 o • Fax ( 9 t 6) :3 2 2 • o 8 8 6 
Victor Wang 
Chairman 
China Silicon Valley 
260 Sheridan A venue, Suite 208 
Palo Alto, CA 94306 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-213 
Dear Mr. Wang: 
September 8, 201 7 
This letter responds to your request for advice as chairman of the China Silicon Valley 
Business Development ("CSV"), a California non-profit corporation, on behalf of Sean Wright, the 
mayor of the City of Antioch, and Rick Kowalczyk, a Half Moon Bay City Councilmember, 
regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the gift and conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common 
Jaw conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should 
change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are payments by the Department of Foreign and Overseas Chinese Affairs, Sichuan 
Provincial People's Government, and by the Foreign Affairs Office of Haikou City, for Mayor 
Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk's trip to China subject to the gift limits set forth in the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
The travel, lodging and meal payments for each official from the Department of Foreign and 
Overseas Chinese Affairs, Sichuan Provincial People's Government, and by the Foreign Affairs 
Office of Haikou City are reportable gifts that are not subject to the gift limits pursuant to Section 
89506(a)(2). 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-213 
Page No. 2 
CSV has invited Mayor Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk to travel to China as part of 
the "September 2017 China Trip." You state that the purpose, sponsorship, and certain 
arrangements for this trip are similar to the Silicon Valley Mayors' China Trips previously 
organized by CSV, including the recent "May 2017 China Trip." We have issued letters regarding 
these trips and other China trips organized by CSV.2 
CSV aims to improve business cooperation between China and the Silicon Valley, help 
create jobs, and increase economic activity in both China and the Silicon Valley. CSV's purpose in 
organizing this trip is to facilitate investment and international trade, and to promote 
communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. Mayor Wright and Councilmember 
Kowalczyk are scheduled to arrive in China on September 10, 2017. They are scheduled to visit 
Chengdu City, Sichuan Province, from September 11 - 14, 2017, and to visit Haikou City, Hianan 
Province, from September 15 - 17, 2017. They will return from China on September 18, 2017. 
While in China, Mayor Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk will have opportunities to 
participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local 
companies. Specifically, the officials will attend the International Innovation Conference in 
Chengdu City and visit the Haikou Industrial Development Zone in Haikou City. 
You state with respect to financing, that the Department of Foreign and Overseas 
Chinese Affairs, Sichuan Provincial People's Government, and the Foreign Affairs Office of 
Haikou City, local Chinese government authorities, will pay for the following estimated expenses 
for Mayor Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk: 
• All international air travel expenses to and from China; $2,000 per person 
• Remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China; $3,000 per person 
You also state that "CSV will not provide any funds or services with monetary value to the 
elected officials in connection with the September 2017 China Trip. " 
ANALYSIS 
Tl,e Act's Gift Limit Provisi011s: 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as: 
.. . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in 
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official 
status. 
2 Advice Letters, Seem No. A-17-103, and Seem No. A-17-085; lnfonnational Assistance Letters, Seem No. 1-
16-236; No. 1-16-079 and No. 1-15-189, and collective requests for advice Reyes, et al Nos. A-14-071, A-14-072, A-14-
078 and A-14-079. 


File No. A-17-213 
Page No. 3 
To reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by 
local public officials in three ways: 
• Gift Limit of $470: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public 
officials. The current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; 
Regulation 18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all public officials. (Sections 89503(a) and 
87200.) 
• Gift Reporting: So that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the 
Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any 
gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year 
are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 87200 - 87210.) 
• Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a 
governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 
or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to 
the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e); Regulations 18700 and 
18703.4.) 
Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging, and 
subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not subject to the Act's current $470 
gift limit. In this regard, Section 89506(a) provides (in pertinent part) an exception from the gift 
limits for certain travel payments: 
Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply: 
(1) .... 
(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
501 ( c )(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax - exempt status 
under Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. 
Here, the travel is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose and is provided 
by a foreign government. The stated purpose of this trip is to facilitate investment and international 
trade, and promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, 
Mayor Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk will have opportunities to participate in meetings 
with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local companies. The 
Department of Foreign and Overseas Chinese Affairs, Sichuan Provincial People's Government, 


File No. A-17-213 
Page No. 4 
and the Foreign Affairs Office of Haikou City will pay for all international air travel expenses to 
and from China, as well as remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during 
the trip. Consequently, the specified payments are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits 
pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). 3 We discuss the conflict of interest issues further below. 
Please note that any payments made for items other than travel and related lodging and 
subsistence may be considered a reportable gift, subject to the $470 gift limit, and subject to the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act. For all gifts, if the official reimburses the donor or 
donor's intermediary for the gift within 30 days, no gift is considered to have been received or 
accepted. (Regulation 1894 l(c)(3).) 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification 
As mentioned above, a public official who receives gift(s) of $470 or more may have a 
financial conflict ofinterest under the Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.) The official must disqualify 
himself or herself from voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that 
source, if the payment was received or promised to the official within 12 months preceding the 
decision. Therefore, if the public official accepts gifts valued at $470 or more, the official may be 
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting the source of those gifts. 
Nonprofits that Regularly Host Travel for Elected Officials: 
Finally, please be aware that Section 89506(f), a recent statute effective January 1, 2016, 
provides that certain nonprofits that regularly organize and host travel for elected officials must 
disclose certain donors. Your facts indicate that CSV, a nonprofit organization, is not financing the 
public officials' travel expenses. However, if you believe the above provision applies, contact us for 
further advice. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
c3 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, LÆgal Division 
3 Note that Regulation I 8945(a) provides: "The person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source of the 
gift unless that person is acting as an intennediary. The person is acting as an inlennediary for the source of the gift 
when the gift to the official was provided under any of the following conditions: (I) the person receives a payment from 
a source and the payment is made to the official after the source identifies the official as the intended recipient of the 
gift; (2) the person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the payment with the understanding that the 
payment will be used for the sole or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or (3) the person receives a 
payment from a source after the payment was solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a 
gift to the official."

Are payments by the Department of Foreign and Overseas Chinese Affairs, Sichuan 
Provincial People's Government, and by the Foreign Affairs Office of Haikou City, for Mayor 
Wright and Councilmember Kowalczyk's trip to China subject to the gift limits set forth in the Act?
sections.conclusion:
The travel, lodging and meal payments for each official from the Department of Foreign and 
Overseas Chinese Affairs, Sichuan Provincial People's Government, and by the Foreign Affairs 
Office of Haikou City are reportable gifts that are not subject to the gift limits pursuant to Section 
89506(a)(2).
